observations.recommendation:
We commended the Municipality for closely monitoring the granting of CAs and its strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002. However, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE)
a. consider possible withholding of salaries of those officials and employees who persistently refuse to cooperate and did not liquidate their CAs within the timeframe as required by the present regulation;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant (MA) to (i) strictly monitor the CAs granted by requiring the personnel concerned to liquidate their CAs including the unspent balances in their possession within the reglementary period; and (ii) send Demand Letter to officials and employees with unliquidated CAs; and
c. instruct the concerned officials and employees to liquidate their CAs within the reglementary period.
observations.managementAction:
a. Not Withholding of salaries is yet to be considered.
b. The Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) requiring the personnel concerned continuously reminded those with unliquidated CAs verbally.
c. Inquiry with the MA revealed that a Demand Letter was not issued, instead they are reminded verbally.
observations.reason: Considerable delays ranging from two to as long as 181 days were incurred in the liquidation of various CAs.

observations.managementAction:
Drawn JEV dated 07/31/24 to adjust/reverse the improper/erroneous entries.
One-time cleansing of the Due to LGUs and Due to NGAs accounts is yet to be undertaken.
Accounting Office is still in the process of reconciling the balances of the accounts.
Only the SL of Due to NGAS was submitted to the Audit Team (AT).
The MAO regularly update the SL to ensure accurate tracking of transactions.
observations.reason: Delay in the implementation of livelihood project in CY 2024. The Accounting Office is still in the process of reconciling the balances of the accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE:
a. direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) closely monitor and ensure that the LDRRM planned programs/projects/activities are implemented and give due regard to the maximum utilization of the 70% MF for pre-disaster preparedness programs so that the intended purpose is attained/achieved; (ii) include in the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (MDRRMFIP) the list of programs, projects and activities to be undertaken out of the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years under STF; and (iii) furnish the Office of the Auditor with Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF within the reglementary period; and
b. direct the MA to draw the journal entries to (i) revert back the savings from reprogrammed DRRMF-Capital Outlay to GF; (ii) record the transfer of the CYs 2022 and 2023 unexpended/ unobligated balances of the DRRMF-Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses and Quick Response Fund from GF account to STF account in the STF books; and (iii) correct the TL - DRRM beginning balance.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality had fully implemented ten projects, with two ongoing, out of the 12 planned PPAs under MF. The specific PPAs to be implemented out of STF were not identified at an earlier stage. A resolution adopting and approving the programming of unprogrammed LDDRM under STF was issued only on July 10, 2024.
JEV was prepared for the transfer of the CYs 2022 and 2023 unexpended/unobligated balances from GF account to STF account in the STF books. The reversal entry is no longer needed since the fund was already reprogrammed and utilized for calamity-related PPAs.
The MAO recorded a credit entry of ₱404,338.06 which further increased the overstatement.
observations.reason: The specific PPAs to be implemented out of STF were not identified at an earlier stage. A resolution adopting and approving the programming of unprogrammed LDDRM under STF was issued only on July 10, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the MA and the Municipal Treasurer (MT) including all AOs to (a) strictly adhere to the timeframes on the submission of accounts and financial reports; and (b) immediately submit the aforementioned unsubmitted DVs and supporting documents.
observations.managementAction:
Delays were still observed in the submission of accounts.
The MA claimed that their office did not receive the unsubmitted DVs for CYs 2016-2020 from the MTO based on the Report of Checks Issued. He also said that majority of the accounts were submitted after the cut-off date indicated in the Audit Observation Memorandum. Furthermore, he explained that they need to check the attached supporting documents prior to transmittal to the AT. With regard to the preparation of financial reports, the delay was caused by late submission of official receipts by the MTO. The submission largely depends on how fast the accounts are forwarded by the MTO to the MAO. The MA will closely coordinate with the new Acting MT regarding the unsubmitted accounts.
observations.reason: Delays were still observed in the submission of accounts. MA stated they did not receive unsubmitted DVs for CYs 2016-2020 from MTO and that many accounts were submitted after audit cut-off dates.

observations.recommendation: We commended the Management for its compliance with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended. However, we recommended that the LCE to: a. instruct the LGU GFPS to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs to ensure that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals and objectives; b. issue an appropriate policy directive, copy furnished to the DILG Office that conducted the review of the LGU GPB, to ensure the dissemination and implementation of the GPB; and shall mandate the LGU GFPS to: a) provide technical assistance in the implementation of the GPB; b) monitor its implementation; c) ensure the preparation and submission of GAD AR; and d) consolidate reports on the implementation of the GPB; and c. instruct the GFP to submit the documentary requirements supporting the GAD Accomplishment Report.
observations.managementAction: The LGU GFPS will coordinate with the LCE regarding the recommendations of the AT to ensure that the implementation of the GPB is religiously followed as planned.
observations.reason: The GAD AR was not accompanied by the requirements mentioned under Section 4.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

observations.recommendation:
1.15 We recommended that the (a) Municipal Treasurer’s Office
(MTO) determine the nature and source of the unidentified CM amounting to
₱65,270.00 and inform the MAO of the details thereof; and (b) MA adjust the stale
checks, unidentified CM, and deposit in transit amounting to ₱185,219.63, ₱65,270.00
and ₱6,923.65, respectively, to correct the balance of the CIB-LCCA accounts.

2.16 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
a. ensure that the approved bond amount aligns with the CAs granted to SDO
and refrain from granting additional CA if the previous ones were not fully
settled or accounted for. Likewise, consider increasing the amount of bond of
the designated SDOs or designating an additional SDO; and
b. instruct the MA to strictly monitor the CAs granted by requiring the SDOs to
liquidate their CAs including the unspent balances in their possession within
the reglementary period.

3.19 We recommended that Management undertake the following courses of action
a. instruct the MT to (i) intensify its efforts to collect RPT delinquencies; and (ii)
consider the other tax remedies provided in R.A. No. 7160 such as (a) posting
the notice of the delinquency in publicly accessible and conspicuous places in
each barangay of the LGU concerned, (b) publishing the notice of delinquency
once a week or for two (2) consecutive weeks in a newspaper of general
circulation of the Municipality and (c) availing of all legal and administrative
remedies either by administrative action through a levy on real property or
judicial action as a last resort; and
b. instruct the MA to reconcile its records with the MT and prepare the necessary
journal entries to adjust the recorded RPT and SET receivables in the
financial statements to reflect the accurate amount of delinquencies.

4.27 We recommended that the LCE:
a. instruct the MA to (i) draw the necessary journal entries to recognize the
amount of Drugs and Medicines and Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies
Inventory as at year-end amounting to ₱2,069,129.39 and ₱1,532,328.00,
respectively; (ii) record all procurement of inventory supplies in the books of
accounts using the Perpetual Inventory System and maintain SLC for each
type of inventory items; (iii) be more vigilant to ensure that issued supplies and
materials were recorded to appropriate expense accounts; and (iv) recognize
in the books the drugs and medicines received from the PDOHO and PHO by
debiting Drugs and Medicines for Distribution and crediting Grants and
Donations in Kind for ₱1,303,266.14, and record the issuance of these items
accordingly through the necessary journal entries;
b. instruct the GSO and other concerned offices to (a) maintain SC for each item
of inventory; and (b) prepare SSMI to be submitted every week or at least once
a month to the MA together with RIS or equivalent document so that issuances
will be computed and recorded by the latter;
c. Inventory, Appraisal and Disposal Committee create a Technical Working
Group (TWG) that would facilitate the conduct of physical count of inventory
items by type semestrally and will prepare the corresponding RPCI; and
d. instruct MHO to furnish the MAO with copies of PTRs from agencies
providing medicines and other medical supplies, to ensure proper accounting
of donations and fair presentation of inventory, expense, and income accounts.

6.26 We recommended that Management direct the:
a. MA to (i) draw a JEV to record the initial set-up of provision for earned leave
credits in the books of accounts of the ensuing year as recommended below:
Account Name Dr. Cr.
Prior Period Adjustment ₱69,413,298.90
Leave Benefits Payable ₱69,413,298.90
(ii) recognize subsequent recording for Leave Benefits Payable at least
quarterly; and (iii) be guided with the suggested journal entries in accounting
for transactions related to leave benefits as illustrated in Annex C.1.3 of COA
Circular No. 2024-009;
b. HRMO Head to (i) initiate and prioritize the reconciliation and correction of
balances in the employee leave cards; (ii) revisit its process for updating and
maintaining leave cards. Specifically, the revised process should include
regularly updating leave cards and other internal control measures to provide
timely and accurate updates; (iii) strictly comply with the provisions of CSC
MC No. 41, s. 1998 and No. 16, s. 2002 on the monetization of the leave credits
of employees. Likewise, discontinue the practice of allowing the monetization
of sick leave credits unless warranted by the circumstances provided under
CSC MC No. 16, s. 2002;
c. enjoin the MA and the HRM Officer to institute a periodic audit of all
employees’ accumulated leaves before approval and payment of their
respective monetized leave credits; and
d. set aside additional reserve funds for the accumulated compensated leave
credits as of year-end; henceforth, ensure the adequacy of the funds provided
for the leave credits earned thereafter by including the said benefits in the
annual budget.

7.24 We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA and other LGU officials
concerned to:
a. trace and confirm records or documents relative to the Giant Clams Stock
Enhancement involving ₱457,348.00, and return the unused fund to the SA
once identified, otherwise, file a request for write-off, if warranted;
b. prepare the necessary JEV to correct the improperly recorded transaction
related to the fund received from Provincial Treasurer ensuring accurate
classification under "Due to LGUs" instead of "Due to NGAs";
c. make representations with the identified SA to facilitate the proper liquidation
and/or return of unutilized fund transfers; and
d. strictly comply with the stipulations stated in the MOA and avoid delay in the
implementation of the project.

8.21 We commended Management for its significant accomplishments in
implementing development projects for CY 2024 and for ensuring compliance with
Section 287 of R.A. No. 7160 and Item 3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated
November 4, 2020. However, we recommended that the (a) Municipal Engineer
coordinate with the contractor to ensure regular updating of the project status to
comply with the guidelines on information and publicity on PPAs; and (b) MA submit
the unsubmitted DVs and supporting documents to facilitate the audit of the
transactions.

9.38 We recommended that the LCE direct the:
a. LDRRM Officer to (i) closely monitor and ensure that the LDRRM planned
PPAs are implemented and give due regard on the maximum utilization of the
70% MF for pre-disaster preparedness programs so that the intended purpose is
attained/achieved; (ii) include in the MDRRMFIP the list of programs, projects
and activities to be undertaken out of the unexpended LDRRMF of previous
years under STF; and (iii) implement stricter monitoring and regular
inspection of stockpiled goods to avoid spoilage and wastage; and
b. MA to make necessary journal entries to correct the overstated TL-LDRRMF.

10.27 Nevertheless, we recommended that Management: (a) submit the required
locator slips/travel orders and leave applications, if any, to verify the absence of 62
personnel during headcount and ensure proper accountability; (b) submit the payroll,
DTRs and Accomplishment Reports of Casual personnel; and (c) refrain from paying
traveling expenses or approving training participation for COS and JO personnel
unless all conditions specified under Section 3(a) of EO No. 77 s. of 2019 and COA
Resolution No. 2021-044 dated December 28, 2021 are fully satisfied.

11.12 We recommended that the LCE to:
a. instruct the LGU GFPS to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs to
ensure that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals and
objectives;
b. issue an appropriate policy directive, copy furnished to the DILG Office that
conducted the review of the LGU GPB, to ensure the dissemination and
implementation of the GPB; and shall mandate the LGU GFPS to: a) provide
technical assistance in the implementation of the GPB; b) monitor its
implementation; c) ensure the preparation and submission of GAD AR; and
d) consolidate reports on the implementation of the GPB; and
c. instruct the GFP to submit the documentary requirements supporting the
GAD Accomplishment Report.

12.15 We recommended that the LSB (a) submit the quarterly and annual reports
using the SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) on SEF utilization, as
prepared by the Municipal Accountant, to the DepEd CO, through appropriate
channels, copy furnished the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the
DBM and DILG; and (b) regularly update the utilization report of the SEF Budget
posted in the LGU’s bulletin boards for transparency and accountability.

13.8 We recommended that the LCE direct the MA and the MT including all
Accountable Officers to (a) strictly adhere to the timeframes on the submission of
accounts and financial reports; (b) immediately transmit the unsubmitted DVs and
supporting documents; and (c) impose appropriate sanctions against those
officials/employees concerned who have continuously disregarded the set deadline for
the submission of the accounts/reports.

14.7 We commended Management for complying with its duties as withholding and
remitting agent of taxes in accordance with RMC No. 23-2012 dated February 14,
2012. However, we recommended that the Accounting personnel in charge strictly
monitor and determine the exact amount to be remitted each month to avoid
under/over remittances.

15.7 We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
requirements of R.A. No. 8291. However, we recommended that the MAO determine
the exact amount to be remitted each month to avoid under/over remittances to avoid
penalties and surcharges that may incur.

16.7 We commended Management for complying with its duties as withholding and
remitting agent of employees’ premiums and loan amortization in accordance with
HDMF Circular No. 275 dated January 22, 2010. However, we recommended that the
MAO strictly monitor and determine the exact amount to be remitted each month to
avoid under/over remittances.

17.8 We commended the Management for its continuous compliance with the
provisions of R.A. No. 10606 on proper deduction and remittance of PhilHealth
contributions. However, we recommended that the MAO determine the exact amount
to be remitted each month to avoid under/over remittances at the end of each month.

We commended the management for its substantial and compliance with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 relative to the granting of CA for CF. However, we recommended that the MA/Municipal Budget Officer ensure full compliance with the deadline of submission of the CA and supporting documents for audit.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management instruct the:
a. MT to (i) prepare a stratified schedule of PIL every tax period to approximate the gross sales or receipt of each business classification and submit a representation to the Sangguniang Bayan in consultation with the Municipal Treasurer to set up or institutionalize the use of the same; (ii) regularly examine the books of accounts of business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales or receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid pursuant to Section 171 of R.A. No. 7160; (iii) assign personnel at the MTO who are well-equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of books of accounts to enable the said Office to carry on with their mandate efficiently; and (iv) tag all the afore-cited suppliers and contractors with underdeclared sales or receipts and be subject to business tax examination of books of accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees, and charges to be collected;

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Municipality for closely monitoring the granting of CAs
and its strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002. However,
we recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) (a) consider possible
withholding of salaries of those officials and employees who persistently refuse to
cooperate and did not liquidate their CAs within the timeframe as required by the
present regulation; (b) instruct the MA to (i) strictly monitor the CAs granted by
requiring the personnel concerned to liquidate their CAs including the unspent
balances in their possession within the reglementary period; and (ii) send Demand
Letter to officials and employees with unliquidated CAs; and (c) instruct the
concerned officials and employees to liquidate their CAs within the reglementary
period.

We commended the management for its substantial compliance with the provisions
of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 relative to the granting of CA
for CF. However, we recommended that the MA/Municipal Budget Officer ensure full
compliance with the deadline of submission of the CA and supporting documents for
audit.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. advise the MA to (a) draw the necessary journal entries to (i) record the
completed project to Buildings account, (ii) adjust the improperly recorded
depreciation of various PPEs; and (iii) capitalize/recognize the amount of
borrowing cost incurred for qualifying assets financed by proceeds of a
loan from the time the project started up to the completion thereof; and
b. regularly renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the
Municipality with LTO and contracts of insurance with GSIS to avoid
additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages to third
party in case of accidents.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. instruct the MAO to (i) draw the necessary journal entries to reclassify the
completed projects recorded under CIP to the appropriate PPE accounts
and the corresponding Accumulated Depreciation at year-end; and (ii)
coordinate with the MEO the documents relative to the project
Landscaping and Other Development of Botolan New Cemetery and
Botolan People's Plaza, Botolan, Zambales for evaluation and recording of
necessary journal entries; and
b. require the MEO to furnish the MAO with the Municipality’s Quarterly
Reports of GPPAs and its supporting documents to apprise the MAO of
the existence and status of all ongoing and completed infrastructure
projects.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MA to:
a. prepare the necessary JEVs to adjust/reverse the improper/erroneous
entries;
b. undertake a one-time cleansing of the Due to LGUs and Due to NGAs
accounts by following the guidelines and procedures for the write-off of
dormant accounts, specifically those from unknown SA;
c. maintain SL for the Due to LGUs and Due to NGAs accounts under GF
and immediately present the same to the Office of the Auditor for
verification and review;
d. regularly update the SL to ensure accurate tracking of transactions; and
e. strictly comply with the stipulations stated in the MOA and avoid delay in
the implementation of the project.

We recommended that the (a) ME and the MPDC properly plan and closely monitor the
implementation of the development projects to prevent the incurrence of delay; (b) ME to
(i) strictly impose liquidated damages to contractors of future projects in case of delay; and
(ii) coordinate with the contractors to comply with the regular updating of the project status
to comply with the guidelines on information and publicity on PPAs; and (c) MA to submit
the unsubmitted DVs and supporting documents to facilitate the audit of the
transactions.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) closely monitor and ensure that the
LDRRM planned programs/projects/activities are implemented and give
due regard to the maximum utilization of the 70% MF for pre-disaster
preparedness programs so that the intended purpose is attained/achieved;
(ii) include in the MDRRMFIP the list of PPAs to be undertaken out of the
unexpended LDRRMF of previous years under STF; and (iii) furnish the
Office of the Auditor with Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of
DRRMF within the reglementary period; and
b. direct the MA to draw the journal entries to (i) revert back the savings
from DRRMF-CO to GF; (ii) record the transfer of the CYs 2022 and 2023
unexpended/unobligated balances of the DRRMF-MOOE and QRF from
GF account to STF account in the STF books; and (iii) correct the TL -
DRRM beginning balance.

We recommended that the Management direct the MA and the MT including
all AOs to (a) strictly adhere to the timeframes on the submission of accounts and
financial reports; and (b) immediately submit the aforementioned unsubmitted DVs
and supporting documents.

We recommended that the LCE (a) ensure that all funds received from SAs
are supported with duly signed MOA/TA to clearly define the specific guidelines and
procedures in the implementation of individual projects, including the responsibilities
of the Municipality, thereby ensuring timely accomplishment thereof for the benefit of
the intended beneficiaries; and (b) impose sanctions on delayed completion of the
projects in the form of liquidated damages.

We recommended that the Management instruct the:
a. MT to (i) prepare a stratified schedule of PIL every tax period to
approximate the gross sales or receipt of each business classification and
submit a representation to the SB to set up or institutionalize the use of the
same; (ii) regularly examine the books of accounts of business
establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales or
receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid pursuant to Section
171 of R.A. No. 7160; (iii) assign personnel at the MTO who are well-
equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of
books of accounts to enable the said Office to carry on with their mandate
efficiently; and (iv) tag all the afore-cited suppliers and contractors with
underdeclared sales or receipts and be subject to examination of books of
accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees, and charges to be
collected;
b. LO to require the submission of complete documentation of the financial
records from taxpayers for the correct evaluation/assessment of business
taxes; and
c. LCE to return the assessment and collection of business taxes to the proper
personnel while taking into account that the examination of books and
pertinent records, as well as utilization of PILAA, are the MT’s
authority/responsibility under the LGC and the existing Municipal RRC
until the existing laws and related ordinances are amended by respective
legislative authorities.

We recommended that the Management:
a. strictly implement the mandatory segregation of wastes at source through
(i) more stringent implementation of the existing ordinance by imposing
fines and penalties for violators, and (ii) conducting a more extensive
information education campaign that would educate and encourage the
active participation of all the constituents on proper waste segregation;
b. ensure that waste collectors/segregators are properly equipped with
personal protective equipment to free them from hazards of handling
solid wastes; and
c. undertake proper information dissemination like conducting seminars
and production of posters/tarpaulins on the harmful effects to public
health of the smoke from burned solid wastes.

We commended the Management for its substantial implementation of the
GAD-related PPAs for CY 2023. Nevertheless, we recommended that the GFPS
ensure that GAD AR is supported with the requirements mentioned under Section
4.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We commended the Management for complying with its duties as withholding
and remitting agent of taxes in accordance with RMC No. 23-2012 dated February
14, 2012. However, we recommended that the Accounting personnel in charge (i)
expedite the reconciliation of ₱46,907.65 to avoid the accumulation of discrepancies
and ensure an accurate balance of the Due to BIR account as of year-end; and (ii)
strictly monitor and determine the exact amount to be remitted each month to avoid
under/over remittances.

We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
requirements of R.A. No. 8291. However, we recommended that the MAO exert extra
effort to reconcile the unreconciled balance of ₱426,995.57 and determine the exact
amount to be remitted each month to avoid under/over remittances.

We commended the Management for complying with its duties as withholding
and remitting agent of employees’ premiums and loan amortization in accordance
with HDMF Circular No. 275 dated January 22, 2010. However, we recommended
that the Accounting personnel in charge to (i) expedite the reconciliation of
₱19,059.60 to avoid the accumulation of discrepancies and ensure an accurate balance
of the Due to Pag-IBIG account as of year-end; and (ii) strictly monitor and determine
the exact amount to be remitted each month to avoid under/over remittances.

We commended the Management for its continuous compliance with the
provisions of R.A. No 10606 on proper deduction and remittance of PhilHealth
contributions. However, we recommended that the MAO exert extra effort to
reconcile the unreconciled balance of ₱1,841.46 and determine the exact amount to
be remitted each month to avoid under/over remittances at the end of each month.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned personnel to (i) exert efforts to improve its implementation of PPAs under the 20% DF to fully attain the LGU’s socio-economic targets and outcomes; (ii) stop the practice of covering the contractors’ delay in the implementation of projects and strictly impose liquidated damages to contractors of future projects in case of delay; and (iii) compute for the amount of liquidated damages to be collected from the contractors of two projects pursuant to Section 68 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer (MT) to (i) intensify efforts on collection to increase RPT and SEF collection efficiency; (ii) consider the other tax remedies provided in R.A. No. 7160 such as (a) posting the notice of the delinquency in publicly accessible and conspicuous places in each barangay of the LGU concerned, (b) publishing the notice of delinquency once a week or for two (2) consecutive weeks in a newspaper of general circulation of the Municipality and (c) availing of all legal and administrative remedies either by administrative action through a levy on real property or judicial action as a last resort; and (iii) furnish the MA of a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for the year to serve as the basis of recording; and
b. direct the MA to (i) make use of the Certified List of Taxpayers and Taxes Due to be submitted by the MT as the basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Credits at the beginning of the year; (ii) make necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatement in the accounts; and (iii) trace and record the necessary adjustments to reconcile the discrepancies between the amount of the recorded RPT and SET Receivables and the related Deferred Credits accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. HRMO to (i) execute a CoS, MOA, or JO Contract with the CoS and JO personnel to clearly define their duties and responsibilities and effectivity date of employment, wage rate, funding charges, place of assignment and acknowledgment of the JO concerned. (ii) determine the whereabouts and submit reason or justification for non-appearance of subject personnel during the headcount. Likewise, require the said unaccounted personnel, if not yet terminated, to appear to the Office of the Auditor to validate their existence; (iii) implement stricter control mechanisms in the monitoring of the attendance of JO personnel to ensure that they remain productive in their respective area of assignments within the designated time schedule of duty; (iv) revisit and evaluate the responsibilities and job descriptions of employees to avoid possible duplication of work and ensure that they are assigned to offices which are in dire need of their services; and (v) prepare a complete Organizational Structure and Staffing Pattern of the LGU so that the necessity and propriety of hiring the CoS and JO personnel are justified; and
b. MA and MT to ensure that the claims for services of CoS and JO personnel are supported with complete and appropriate documentation.
observations.managementAction:
JO Contracts/Appointments were prepared stipulating the duties and responsibilities, effectivity date of employment, wage rate, funding charges, place of assignment and acknowledgment of the JO concerned.

A Memorandum dated March 6, 2023, was issued by the Municipal Mayor directing the JO and CoS personnel to submit individual justifications/reasons for non-appearance during the headcount last December 21 and 22, 2022 and to personally appear before the COA office for validation purposes. 18 out of 45 were already interviewed by the Audit Team on various dates.

Strict monitoring of attendance is imposed by logging the entrance and exit of JO personnel in the logbook of each office signed by their immediate supervisors and locator slips were also implemented for those who have assigned tasks outside of the office.

The responsibilities and job descriptions of employees were revisited. The JO personnel were instructed to submit their actual duties and responsibilities.

Justification was submitted that it would be difficult to forecast the number of JO and CoS to be hired per Office due to multifarious services which must be delivered and work programs that need to be implemented at varying times.

The claims for services of CoS and JO personnel are supported with complete and appropriate documentation.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the:
MA to (i) maintain SLC for each commodity/stock; (ii) record all procurement of inventory supplies in the books of accounts using the Perpetual Inventory System; (iii) draw the necessary journal entries to derecognize the amount of Welfare Goods Supplies amounting to ₱2,480,000.00; (iv) draw the necessary journal entries to adjust the correct amount of Drugs and Medicines Inventory, Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies, Office Supplies and Other Office Supplies and Materials Inventory as at year-end amounting to ₱1,596,240.50, ₱1,514,114.00, ₱228,404.00 and ₱40.00, respectively; and (v) be more vigilant to ensure that issued supplies and materials were recorded to appropriate expense accounts; and
GSO to (i) maintain stock cards for each item of inventory; (ii) regularly reconcile the stock cards with the SLCs of the MAO; (iii) report the issuances of supplies thru the SSMI supported with RIS and/or proof of distribution of goods and supplies and furnish a copy thereof to the MA which shall be the basis in recording to appropriate Expenses accounts; and (iv) submit the physical count of stocks of all supplies and materials reports to the Office of the Auditor and other offices concerned on a semi-annual basis.

2.20	We recommended that Management instruct the MA to draw adjusting entries to correct the misstatements of the PPE.
2.21	The MA committed to comply with the recommendations.

3.14	We recommended that Management instruct the MHO and staff with the coordination of the Human Resource Management Officer and MA to submit the list of eligible frontliners duly approved by the Local Health Board, DTRs, duties and responsibilities with accomplishment reports and Appointments/plantilla of casuals, JOs and BHWs for us to evaluate the validity and propriety of the grant and disbursement of the AHDP and SRA.
3.15	We also recommended that Management require the concerned employees to refund the amount of ₱45,000.00 representing the excessive amount of AHDP received and submit a legal basis in the grant of AHDP to National Government personnel in order to validate the legality and validity of payment of AHDP.
3.16 	The MHO committed to submit the necessary documents relative to the payment of AHDP. Likewise, the Chief of Hospital, in his written reply requested for a longer period to complete the refund of excessive AHDP as these will be deducted from the salary of the personnel concerned for an average period of three months.

4.29	We recommended that Management:
a.	coordinate with the MSWDO to set well-defined policies and clear-cut guidelines on screening, verification, assessment and documentary requirements to promote more equitable cremation assistance;
b.	instruct the MA to stop the practice of withholding 5% VAT on payments made for deceased Senior Citizens; and
c.	instruct the partner crematorium to (i) refund excessive amounts billed totaling to at least ₱48,000.00 without prejudice to the result of Management’s further evaluation and analysis of the matter on hand; and (ii) effect mandatory 20% discount and VAT exemption on transactions involving Senior Citizens to avoid sanctions provided under Section 7 of RA No. 9994.

5.17	We recommended and Management agreed to:
	direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) include in the MDRRMFIP the list of programs, projects and activities to be undertaken out of the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years; and (ii) furnish the Office of the Auditor with a Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF within the reglementary period; and
	instruct the MA to (i) transfer immediately the unexpended balance for CY 2020 amounting to ₱273,933.65 to a STF account; (ii) submit the aforementioned vouchers and supporting documents immediately to the Office of the Auditor; and (iii) perform a thorough review of all disbursement claims and ensure only those with complete documentation are processed and presented for payment.

6.18	We recommended and Management agreed to:
	instruct the BAC to (i) implement the projects on the date specified in the 20% Development Fund Plan; (ii) ensure that the timeframe for each procurement stage/activity has been strictly followed; and (iii) require the DOLE approved CSHP before contract preparation and signing;
	direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) coordinate with the contractors to comply with the required posting of information and publicity on PPAs of government agencies and to inform the Audit Team within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted at the project site; and (ii) strictly monitor the implementation of succeeding projects to see to it that the same are completed within the period of duration of the contracts; and
	advise the MA to (i) impose liquidated damages against the contractors of the projects discussed on Items 6.8 and 6.9 upon payment thereof; and (ii) submit the aforementioned vouchers and supporting documents immediately to the Office of the Auditor to determine the propriety of the disbursements made.

7.15	We recommended and Management agreed to:
	continuously comply with the GAD mechanisms and processes to sustain the LGU’s critical consciousness in supporting gender and development, women’s empowerment and responding to gender issues; and
	ensure that GAD AR is supported with the requirements mentioned under Section 5.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
7.16	The management commented in their written reply that the MSWDO had difficulty in the implementation of youth programs due to limited movement during pandemic. Likewise, youths are busy with their learning modules, had fear of contacting COVID-19 and frequent rescheduling of meetings led to poor attendance during youth meeting. Moreover, in the celebration of National Children’s Month, not all barangays participated due to protocols at that time and parents were hesitant to allow minors to go out for the program. Although the LGU may have failed to fully utilize the GAD funds, the allocated resources were redirected to address a more urgent concern and for the best interest of the public.

8.10	We recommended that management instruct the MA, Municipal Treasurer (MT) and BAC Chairman to (a) immediately transmit to the Audit Team the unsubmitted accounts and financial reports; and (b) strictly comply to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submission of financial reports including copies of perfected contracts/purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 and other pertinent issuances.
8.11	The MA commented during our exit conference that another reason for the delayed submission is the transfer of files to the new Municipal Building. Likewise, the LGU already hired and trained one staff as a replacement for the one who resigned. On the other hand, the MT averred that sometimes the check is being prepared even though some of the documents were not signed by proper officials. This is done in order not to delay transactions, especially since the implementation of LGAM requires more forms and signatories; thus, completion of the signatories was made before forwarding the DVs to the MAO.

9.8	We recommended that Management instruct the:
	MA and MT determine the exact details of the balances to identify the amount to be remitted or adjusted for the GF and TF books and if necessary, hire/assign an employee who will perform such function or assist the personnel-in-charge to facilitate the process thereof; and
	MA see to it that fund transfers from SEF and TF were made to the GF before remittances of taxes be made, and reconcile the balances of the Due to BIR account monthly, to arrive at an accurate balance of the account, for fair presentation in the Financial Statements.
9.9	The management committed to comply with the recommendations.

10.9	We commended the efforts of the MAO and MTO and recommended to continue to be compliant with regulations on withholding and remittance of monthly PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period. We also recommended that both Offices investigate/trace the difference of ₱2,172.23 and remit the same to PhilHealth. Likewise, see to it that all the contributions withheld in a given month were remitted in full to avoid accumulation of balances and ensure an accurate balance of the Due to PhilHealth account as of year-end.
10.10	The management committed to comply with the recommendations.

11.5	We recommended that Management instruct the MAO and MTO to (a) exert extra efforts to verify the unaccounted deductions of ₱187,660.94 and remit the same to the GSIS; and (b) maintain their good practice of religiously observing the prescribed deadlines on the withholding of premiums/deductions and the respective remittance thereof to the GSIS pursuant to the GSIS Act of 1997.
11.6	The management committed to comply with the recommendations.

12.8	We appreciated the effort of Management for withholding and remittance of premiums and loan amortizations to Pag-IBIG within the prescribed period. However, we recommended that the MA and the Accounting staff in charge of Pag-IBIG remittances trace and reconcile the difference to ensure an accurate balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account as of year-end.
12.9	The management committed to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that management (a) instruct the MSWDO to plan prudently the utilization of the funds and implement fully the procurement process for the attainment of the desired benefit of the programs/projects; and (b) direct the Municipal Treasurer and MSWDO to ensure presentation of IDs or CTCs by the claimants, i.e. pensioners and workers before release of payment for confirmation of their identity and utmost protection of the interest of the Municipal Government; and (c) require the Municipal Accountant and MSWDO to scrutinize the supporting documents to ascertain their completeness before the processing of any disbursements.

We recommended that management (a) direct the BAC and Technical Working Group (TWG) (i) to exercise utmost diligence in performing their duties in all phases of the procurement, particularly in handling pre-qualification and post-qualification procedures in order to arrive at a more accurate/appropriate decision; (ii) submit justification for not rejecting ineligible documents presented by supplier/contractor during the post-qualification stage;  (iii) comply with the amendments of the Bid Securing Declaration and Omnibus Sworn Statement pursuant to the RIRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that funds are available before executing any contract to prevent incurrence of liability for void contract.

We recommended that management (a) direct the BAC and Technical Working Group (TWG) to fully scrutinize all eligibility documents submitted by the supplier in order to arrive at an accurate/appropriate decision; and (b) submit an explanation why I.L. Fernando General Merchandise was declared as the Single or Lowest Calculated and Responsive Quotation knowing that the eligibility requirements presented for evaluation were not appropriate and relevant.

We recommended that management:
centralize the hiring of job order, contractual and honoraria-based personnel at the HRMO to ensure the completeness and reliability of records.
reduce the number of job order/contractual personnel to the bare essential based on actual project/program needs;
instruct the Personnel Officer to require job order personnel to submit verified/certified accomplishment reports to support the propriety of the payment of their wages; and maintain an updated and complete records (201 files) for the job order, contractual and honoraria-based personnel for reference purposes; and direct the Personnel Officer and Heads of Offices concerned to submit their explanation for (i) allowing payment of wages out of Municipal fund of those personnel who render services to Camp Kainomayan, a private company and the driver of private vehicles transporting to Mt. Pinatubo and Tukal-Tukal Falls; and (ii) absence of action against 279 personnel who failed to appear during the head count conducted by the Audit Team.

We recommended that management (a) consider the DENR survey on high risk areas in setting priorities in the implementation of projects to ensure reduction of disaster risk and enhancement of disaster preparedness; (b) provide livelihood/relief assistance to disaster victims instead of cash in accordance with Section 5.3 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; (c)  instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to review the disbursements and items of expenditures for propriety of charging to LDRRMF before certifying the existence of appropriation; and (d) require the Municipal Agriculturist to submit thru the Municipal Accountant photographs of the damage to support his validation on the typhoon affected area.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) require the GFPS to (i) discharge their functions faithfully pursuant to Executive Order No. 8 and the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-001 as amended; (ii) adopt the HGDG in the identification of real gender issues in the Municipality; and (iii) create a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team to monitor efficiently and effectively and evaluate the outcomes of the LGU’s GAD Policies and Programs. These measures will enable the Municipality to comply with the mandate of R.A. 9710 or the Magna Carta of Women; thereby, promoting a gender responsive governance.

We recommended that management (a) comply fully with the provision of protective gears and supplies to garbage sorters and collectors; (b) intensity information drive on proper waste segregation by providing properly labeled trash cans, signages and billboards; (c) monitor closely the SWM personnel assigned at the Municipal MRF in Barangay Panan to ensure proper waste disposal; (d) find an appropriate location as containment area for the residual wastes to prevent harmful effects to human beings and environment; (e) compel all barangays to enforce the Municipal SWM and to participate actively in all programs, projects and activities of the Municipality for the successful implementation of the 10-year SWM plan, particularly the proper segregation at source and disposal of wastes at the designated MRF; and (f) impose garbage fees for the collection of wastes of all institutions, establishments, and households to generate additional funds for the implementation of SWM, and enforce charging of fines and penalties to violators of prohibited acts as a deterrent mechanism.

We reiterated our recommendation that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the taxes withheld are remitted in full to the Bureau of Internal Revenue within the timeframe to avoid the possible incurrence of interest and penalty.

We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to process early the remittance vouchers to meet payment deadlines and avoid imposition of interest charges in compliance with PhilHealth Circular No. 0001.

We recommended that the management:

inform the contractor awarded with the Provision of Potable Water System Project to expedite the execution of the project and finish the same within the contract duration of 120 days as stipulated in the Contract of Agreement; otherwise, liquidated damages shall be imposed to the contractor for the delayed completion thereof; and
require the BAC to (i) conduct pre-procurement process for projects costing above ₱5,000,000.00 to clear out issues and concerns prior to conduct of bidding, specifically on the timeframe; (ii) revise the format of the Certification regarding the posting of ITB in conspicuous places to include all essential information; and 


(iii) comply with the amendments of the Bid Securing Declaration and Omnibus Sworn Statement pursuant to the RIRR of R.A. No. 9184.

constant monitoring of projects to preclude recurrence of unutilized funds;
(b) efficiently and effectively execute the projects as planned to obtain
maximum benefits therefrom; (c) revisit the 20% Development Plan and
align PPAs along the perspective of the JMC of the DILG/DBM in order to
ensure quality project outcomes that promise long-term benefits to the
constituents; (d) require all or majority of BAC members to
decide/certify/sign the Abstracts of Quotation, in case of procurement thru
shopping and other alternative modes; and (e) oblige all bidders to include in
their omnibus sworn statements a complete testimony to prove their integrity
as contractors/suppliers in accordance with the requirements of Section 25.3
of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184.

3.8 We recommended that the management:
a. carefully plan on how to achieve the ideal and realistic number of
personnel complement of the Municipality; consider filling up funded
vacant permanent positions to resolve the problem on lack of personnel;
and reduce number of job order/contractual personnel to the bare
essential based on actual project/ program needs;
b. instruct the Personnel Officer to (1) prepare appointments for the
honoraria-based personnel and require barangay employees receiving
additional honorarium in the Municipality to submit copies of their
approved appointments from their respective Barangays to serve as
agreement for services to be rendered; (2) necessitate the submission of
verified/certified accomplishment reports, certified copy of logbook to
substantiate actual service; and supporting documents for all payments
of wages and honoraria; and (3) maintain records (201 files) for all
contractual, job order and honoraria-based personnel for reference
purposes; and
c. direct the Personnel Officer and Heads of Offices to prepare statements
of duties and responsibilities of individual job order personnel in
accordance with the objectives of the Office concerned as benchmark in
the evaluation of their tasks; submit explanation on the absence of five
personnel during the head count to facilitate rendition of appropriate
audit action/decisions thereof.

4.6 We recommended that the management (a) instruct the focal persons of the
different programs/projects to: (i) fully implement and prudently plan the
utilization of the funds transferred from NGAs and strictly monitor the
implementation thereof in accordance with the salient terms and conditions
of the MOAs; (ii) coordinate with the OIC-Municipal Engineer regarding the
renovation of the room for Trichoderma Production Laboratory at Techno
Pinoy Building to achieve the desired result/output of the proposed project;
and (iii) for future projects, ensure that all factors are considered during the
planning stage to include information relative to the time frame of
implementation, projected outputs, activities to be undertaken for a given
period, persons accountable, applicable mode of procurement to be used, and
program of works for construction projects; and (b) direct the Municipal
Accountant to provide a checklist as guide in the processing of disbursement
claims and make sure that payments are not allowed unless documentation is
complete and appropriate.

5.13 We recommended the management (a) carefully plan the projects, programs
and activities that may be included in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Plan to see to it that only those items that are sanctioned and
authorized by R.A. No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1
dated March 25, 2013 are included therein to prevent illegal and irregular
disbursement of public funds; and (b) consider providing additional
permanent personnel under the LDRRMO to fully perform the three basic
functions geared towards efficient and effective conduct of training, research
and planning, and operation and warning.

6.31 We recommended that the management:
a. give priority and enforce the implementation of the existing Ordinance
on the Municipal Solid Waste Management for the attainment of the
commitment of the LGU towards environmental protection and effective
solid waste management;
b. compel all barangays to enforce the Municipal SWM and actively
participate in all programs, projects and activities of the Municipality for
the successful implementation of the ten-year SWM plan and ensure
proper segregation and disposal of wastes;
c. settle down with the Director of DOST the problems encountered in the
operation of the machines and comply with the respective obligations
stipulated in the MOA inasmuch as the machines are almost paid;
d. appropriate sufficient funds for the purchase of dump truck to solve the
problem on the collection of residual wastes to other Barangays,
materials for the operation of machines, and lacking equipment of SWM
personnel;
e. issue memorandum to all SWM personnel giving instructions on their
daily target of performing duties and responsibilities such as collection,
segregation and disposal of wastes, and operation of the machine to
resolve voluminous wastes coming into the MRF, and require garbage
personnel on the constant wearing of protective equipment for their own
safety;
f. find an appropriate location as containment area for the residual wastes
to prevent harmful effects to human beings and environment;
g. provide cover on garbage truck to prevent scattering of wastes and
spreading of awful odor, and imprint telephone number on the body of
74
the dump truck in case of notification for immediate pick-up of wastes;
provide labels on trash bins to inform users about segregation of wastes;
and signages in areas to make constituents aware of the strict
implementation of the SWM;
h. impose garbage fees to all institutions, establishments, and households
for the collection of wastes to generate additional funds for the
implementation of SWM, and fines and penalties to violators of the
prohibited acts to enforce the Municipal SWM; and
i. require the Sanggunian to revisit the Ordinance to amend the provision
that was inadvertently omitted particularly the prescribed period and
frequency of collection of garbage fees under Section 1 of Article VIII, as
well as the fines and penalties enforceable against prohibited acts under
paragraphs 2 and 3 of Section 2, Article IX thereof.

7.17 We recommended that management (a) require every League of Local
Government Units, and League and Federation of Local Elective Officials to
submit complete supporting documents prior to any release of financial aid;
(b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the documents are
complete before processing any claim thereof; and (c) stop any payment to
private organization where municipal employees are members or affiliates
thereto using the Municipal funds; otherwise, the Audit Team shall be
constrained to disallow the same.

8.21 We recommended management to:
a. strictly enforce the existing rules and regulations in the operation of all
motor vehicles including tricycles in the Municipality;
b. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to impose correct permit fees in
accordance with the Revenue Code. Scrutinize the requirements and
have the checklist certified to ensure that only qualified drivers/operators
are issued permits or franchise. At least once a month, reconcile records
with the different sectors to determine registered active and inactive
members, as against the records maintained in the Office of the Vice
Mayor. Maintain registry of all tricycle operators which includes the
names and addresses, contact numbers and brands of tricycle, route,
sector, body number together with an orderly file of supporting
documents for reference and control;
c. assign personnel to conduct inspection of the tricycles to conform with the
minimum standard of physical requirement/equipage;
d. request the Chairman of the Committee on Transportation, and Peace,
Order and Security to revisit the Resolution/Ordinances issued for
effective regulation of operation of tricycles and implementation of the
traffic rules and regulations;
e. conduct meeting with the PNP, barangays and different sectors (TODA)
to enforce rules and regulations and resolve issues and concerns; and
f. take into consideration the hiring/assigning of additional task force
personnel to enforce the traffic rules and regulations, and install CCTV
for monitoring and recording purposes as one way to control colorum
vehicles as well as promoting public safety and order.

9.7 We recommended that management (a) confer with the concerned donors to
enter into deed of donation duly supported by the CR/OR and other related
documents to facilitate filing of application for insurance of the remaining 12
vehicles; and (b) allocate budget for the insurance premiums due each
vehicle.

10.6 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant
submit the accounts and reports for various months and adhere strictly to
the timeframes on the submission of accounts and financial reports pursuant
to Section 122 of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular Nos. 2009-006 and 95-006.

12.6 We recommended that the management require the Municipal Treasurer
and Municipal Accountant to exercise strict adherence to Section 14 of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations of the Government Service Insurance
System Act of 1997 to avoid the possible incurrence of interest and penalty.

We recommended that the management file an action before the appropriate court, serve written extrajudicial demand to the borrowers, or secure written acknowledgement of the debt from the debtor to interrupt the running of the prescriptive period to collect said livelihood loan. Further, install an internal control to monitor the collections of debts payable to the municipality as they fall due.

Furthermore, refrain from issuing clearance from financial accountabilities to officials and employees who were granted said livelihood loan and remained unpaid at the date of their retirement, unless and until their respective debts are settled.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer sent demand letters to debtors.




Personnel separated from the Municipality (3 appointed and 3 elected officials) were not among the listed debtors with existing unpaid loan.
observations.reason:
Out of ₱1,840,401.50 loans on livelihood only 1.28% was collected. Management did not file a case against the delinquent debtors.  

The Municipal Treasurer failed to install control measures to monitor collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management designate a municipal official and/or employee that will oversee the compliance of the local government with the salient terms and conditions of MOAs and to faithfully and fully implement the same within the parameters therein stated on funds received from NGAs to ensure that the purposes of the fund transfer are properly, promptly, and faithfully achieved. Furthermore, release the material assistance it purchased in the amount of ₱14,734.85 to Paco People’s Handicraft Association.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor issued a Memorandum Order creating a Project Monitoring Team to oversee the implementa-tion of fund transfers from NGAs.




The materials assistance were issued as evidenced by the acknowledge-ment of receipt by Paco People’s Handicraft Association on April 13, 2016.
observations.reason:
The Project Monitoring Team supervised and monitored the execution of projects; however, there were still unimplemented projects in CY 2016.  

Reiterated in Observation No. 4, Part II of the CY 2016 AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal School Board exercise care in identifying projects, programs and activities that are in line with the purposes for the creation and collection of the SEF in accordance with Section 1 of RA No. 5447, Section 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991, DECS – DBM – DILG Joint Circular Nos. 01 – B dated June 25, 2001, 01 – A dated March 14, 2000 and 01 dated April 14, 1998.

Furthermore, refrain the practice of charging expenses for local travel, training, office supplies, electricity, donations, and other maintenance and operating expenses, purchase of LED television, photocopier machine and gas stove, payment of school papers, internet connections, financial assistance and the like to the SEF, otherwise the audit team will be forced to issue the corresponding Notice of Disallowance to require the restitutions of the said amounts.

Finally, if the same expense cannot be avoided, charge the said expenses to the General Fund proper.
observations.managementAction:
A Local School Board meeting was called on March 10, 2016 requiring all school heads to revise their proposed budget in accordance with the SEF Circulars.




Before signing the Disbursement Vouchers and supporting documents, officials ensured that expenses are in pursuance of the SEF Circulars.

Expenses not in compliance with the Circulars were stopped for charging to SEF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management carefully plan the projects, programs and activities that may be included in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Plan to see to it that only those items that are sanctioned and allowed by R.A. No. 10121 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012 – 73 (A) dated April 17, 2012 are included therein and prevent illegal and irregular disbursement of public funds.Furthermore, it should strictly comply with the existing guidelines in the purchase of motor vehicles.

Failure to do so may warrant the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance.
observations.managementAction: The Report on Utilization of LDRRMF has been adjusted and its correspond-ing RAAO.  Cost of the motor vehicle was included in the unexpended balance and was transferred to the Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management revisit its Annual Budget for CY 2016 to see to it that it appropriated sufficient funds to comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 9003 including the purchase of new and additional garbage trucks and related equipment for garbage collection and transportation considering existing laws, rules and regulations.

Furthermore, install measures to address the issues (a) determined as causes/reasons for the partial operation of the MRF and (b) identified during the pilot testing of barangay – based waste collection in order to fully implement the barangay – based waste collection.
observations.managementAction: Additional appropriation was approved for the payment of hauling and disposal of wastes at Metro Clark instead of procurement of garbage truck.  Likewise, supplies were purchased for the SWM personnel.
observations.reason:
The procurement of dump truck and other equipment for SWM personnel was not prioritized.  

Reiterated in Observation No. 6, Part II of the CY 2016 AAR.

Lapses in the implementation of Municipal SWM were still noted.  Reiterated in Observation No.  6, Part II of the CY 2016 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management refrain from hiring legal consultant except in those cases provided under paragraph 3(1), Section 481 of the Local Government Code of 1991. We also required the submission of documents to substantiate the claim of LAC for the services he rendered to the Municipality.
